segments.text: Do you know when the mountain goats give birth? Do you watch when the doe bears her fawn?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: Do you count the months till they bear? Do you know the time they give birth?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: They crouch down and bring forth their young; their labor pains are ended.
Their young thrive and grow strong in the wilds; they leave and do not return.
Who let the wild donkey go free? Who untied its ropes?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: I gave it the wasteland as its home, the salt flats as its habitat.
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: It laughs at the commotion in the town; it does not hear a driver's shout.
It ranges the hills for its pasture and searches for any green thing.
Will the wild ox consent to serve you? Will it stay by your manger at night?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: Can you hold it to the furrow with a harness? Will it till the valleys behind you?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: Will you rely on it for its great strength? Will you leave your heavy work to it?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: Can you trust it to haul in your grain and bring it to your threshing floor?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: The wings of the ostrich flap joyfully, though they cannot compare with the wings and feathers of the stork.
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: She lays her eggs on the ground and lets them warm in the sand,
unmindful that a foot may crush them, that some wild animal may trample them.
She treats her young harshly, as if they were not hers; she cares not that her labor was in vain,
for God did not endow her with wisdom or give her a share of good sense.
Yet when she spreads her feathers to run, she laughs at horse and rider.
Do you give the horse its strength or clothe its neck with a flowing mane?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: Do you make it leap like a locust, striking terror with its proud snorting?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: It paws fiercely, rejoicing in its strength, and charges into the fray.
It laughs at fear, afraid of nothing; it does not shy away from the sword.
The quiver rattles against its side, along with the flashing spear and lance.
In frenzied excitement it eats up the ground; it cannot stand still when the trumpet sounds.
At the blast of the trumpet it snorts,
Aha!
segments.speaker: Horse

segments.text: Does the hawk take flight by your wisdom and spread its wings toward the south?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: Does the eagle soar at your command and build its nest on high?
segments.speaker: God

segments.text: It dwells on a cliff and stays there at night; a rocky crag is its stronghold.
From there it looks for food; its eyes detect it from afar.
Its young ones feast on blood, and where the slain are, there it is.
